basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Kawthar — “Abundant Good” — is the shortest sūra of the Quran: three verses only. It takes its name from the gift promised to the Prophet in the first verse, al-Kawthar (الكَوْثَر), “the Abundant”. Tradition reports that it was revealed in response to a man of Quraysh, al-ʿĀṣ ibn Wāʾil, who said of the Prophet: “Leave him, he is a man without posterity; when he dies, no one will speak of him again.” The Prophet's two sons, Qāsim and ʿAbd Allāh, had died as toddlers; the word abtar (أَبْتَر), “cut off”, was then used of a man whose male line was extinguished. The sūra turns the insult back: it is the enemy of the Prophet who is abtar. And in place of a posterity of children, a river is given to him — the Kawthar — sign of a deeper abundance, whose spiritual lineage cannot be measured.

versets.trad: Truly We have bestowed abundant good upon you.
versets.commentaire: al-Kawthar (الكَوْثَر) derives from kathīr (كَثِير), “much”, “abundant”: it is abundance itself. The commentators have given it more than sixteen meanings: a river or a pool in Paradise, the gift of prophecy, the Quran itself, the daily prayer, the profession of faith, the light that guides to religion, the Prophet's posterity. A ḥadīth reports that during his ascension the Prophet saw a river “whose banks were tents of hollow pearls”, and Gabriel said to him: “This is the Kawthar.” Al-Ṭabarsī sums up: all these meanings are facets of one good — the abundance God bestows in both abodes, this world and the next.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + suffix pronoun We: “truly, We”.
aʿṭā: to give, to grant, to bestow; perfect 1st pers. plur. + suffix pronoun 2nd pers. masc. sing.: We have given you.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: al-Kawthar: an intensive form of the root k-th-r (abundance, multitude); names abundance itself — often identified with a river in Paradise, but also with every grace given to the Prophet.

versets.trad: So pray to your Lord and offer sacrifice!
versets.commentaire: The answer to the gift is twofold: the ṣalāt (صَلَاة) — ritual prayer — and the sacrifice, naḥr (نَحْر), which in its first sense names the slaughter of an animal (camel or bovine) at the hollow of the neck, the practice of the Feast of Sacrifice. A less common reading hears wa-nḥar as “raise your hands [up to the naḥr, the base of the neck]” — the gesture that accompanies the takbīr at the opening of prayer. In either reading, the response to received abundance is a movement toward God: the prayer that lifts up, the sacrifice that gives back.

mots.def: linking particle: so, then; introduces the consequence of the previous gift.
ṣallā: to pray; imperative, “pray”. Also names the ritual prayer, ṣalāt.
preposition li- + rabb + suffix pronoun: to, addressed to your Lord.

naḥara: to sacrifice by slitting the neck at its base (camel, bovine); imperative, “sacrifice”. Another reading hears: “raise the hands [up to the naḥr, the base of the neck]” at the opening of prayer.

versets.trad: Truly your enemy shall be the one without posterity.
versets.commentaire: shāniʾ (شَانِئ) — the one who hates, who scorns. abtar (أَبْتَر) — literally, the animal whose tail is cut; figuratively, the one whose posterity is broken off, after whom nothing follows. This is the word the Prophet's enemies had used against him, because his sons had died as toddlers. The sūra turns it back: it is the enemy who is abtar, with no true posterity. The Prophet is given the Kawthar — not children by blood, but a spiritual lineage beyond measure. As the Quran says elsewhere: “the Day when neither wealth nor children avail, save for him who comes to God with a sound heart” (26:88–89).

mots.def: shāniʾ: one who hates, who scorns; active participle of shanaʾa. With the suffix pronoun: your enemy, the one who hates you.
personal pronoun 3rd pers. masc. sing.: he; here as a pronoun of separation.
abtar: literally, with a severed tail; figuratively, one whose posterity is extinguished, after whom nothing follows. The word the Prophet's enemies had used against him.
